title: Rewrite all 15 pattern descriptions with problem-first hooks
description: CONCEPT AUDIT: All 15 patterns score 0/3 on T1 (Hook). Every description starts with a GoF textbook definition ('Defines a...', 'Ensures a...', 'Provides a...'). Rewrite each to open with a PROBLEM the student can relate to. Template: 'You're building [concrete app]. [Specific problem arises]. How do you solve it without [bad approach]?' This is the single highest-impact change for teaching quality. EXAMPLE — Observer current: 'Defines a one-to-many dependency between objects...' → Rewrite: 'You're building a stock trading app. When a stock price changes, the dashboard, notifications, and portfolio ALL need to update. Do you check every component manually?'
acceptanceCriteria: All 15 pattern descriptions start with a concrete problem scenario, NOT a definition
Each hook is under 3 sentences and uses 'you' (second person)
The GoF definition is STILL present but moved to after the hook
At least 5 hooks reference real apps (Spotify, Uber, Amazon, Gmail, Slack)
A/B test: show 10 developers both versions, 8/10 prefer the hook version

title: Add intuitive analogy field to all 15 patterns (before UML)
description: CONCEPT AUDIT: All 15 patterns score 0/3 on T2 (Intuition). No pattern has an analogy or concrete example before showing the UML. Add 'analogy' field to DesignPattern type — a 2-3 sentence everyday analogy. EXAMPLE — Observer: 'Think of a newspaper subscription. The newspaper publishes, subscribers receive. No subscriber knows about others. Adding a new subscriber requires zero changes to the newspaper.' Strategy: 'Think of GPS navigation — same destination, different route strategies. You swap routes without changing the car.' Source: refactoring.guru uses analogies for every pattern.
acceptanceCriteria: DesignPattern type has new 'analogy: string' field
All 15 patterns have analogies using everyday objects/experiences
Analogy displayed in Properties panel ABOVE the code sample section
Analogies use concrete nouns (newspaper, restaurant, GPS) not abstract terms
Each analogy maps cleanly to the pattern's key classes (Subject=newspaper, Observer=subscriber)

title: Add complexity/tradeoff intuition text to all 15 patterns
description: CONCEPT AUDIT: All 15 patterns score 0/3 on T5 (Complexity Intuition). No pattern explains the tradeoffs in concrete terms. Add 'tradeoffs: string' field explaining: what you GAIN (decoupling, flexibility, extensibility) and what you PAY (indirection, complexity, memory). Use concrete language. EXAMPLE — Observer: 'You gain: adding new observers without touching existing code. You pay: debugging is harder because you can't see the call chain — events are implicit, not explicit.' Singleton: 'You gain: guaranteed single instance. You pay: global state makes testing harder — you can't mock it easily.'
acceptanceCriteria: DesignPattern type has new 'tradeoffs: string' field
All 15 patterns have concrete tradeoff explanations
Each tradeoff has a 'You gain/You pay' structure
Displayed in Properties panel under pattern description
No abstract jargon — all tradeoffs use concrete examples

title: Restructure SOLID demos to lead with analogy, not definition
description: CONCEPT AUDIT: SOLID demos score 1/3 on T1 and T2 — they have analogies (restaurant, electrical plugs, etc.) but they're BURIED in the 'realWorldExample' field shown at the bottom of Properties panel. Move the analogy to the OPENING of the description. The analogy should be the FIRST thing the student reads, not the last. EXAMPLE — SRP current description: 'A class should have only one reason to change. Each class should encapsulate a single responsibility.' → Restructured: 'In a restaurant, the chef cooks, the waiter serves, and the cashier handles payments. If one person did all three, any change disrupts everything. That's the Single Responsibility Principle: each class does ONE job.'
acceptanceCriteria: All 5 SOLID demo descriptions start with the real-world analogy
The formal principle definition follows the analogy, not the other way around
The 'realWorldExample' field content is integrated into the main description
Properties panel shows analogy prominently at the top

title: Add memorable 3-bullet summary to all 20 patterns and 5 SOLID principles
description: CONCEPT AUDIT: All concepts score 0/3 on T12 (Summary). No pattern or SOLID principle has a concise takeaway. Add 'summary: string[]' field with exactly 3 bullets per concept — what it IS (one sentence), what's the KEY INSIGHT (the aha moment), and WHEN to use it (one trigger sentence). EXAMPLE — Observer: ['Observer = subscribe to changes, get notified automatically', 'Key insight: Subject and Observers don't know about each other → loose coupling', 'Use when: one change should trigger multiple independent reactions']. These become flashcard-ready content.
acceptanceCriteria: DesignPattern type has 'summary: string[]' field (exactly 3 items)
All 20 patterns have 3-bullet summaries
All 5 SOLID demos have 3-bullet summaries
Summaries displayed at bottom of Properties panel in a highlighted card
Each bullet is under 15 words — flashcard-sized
Bullets follow the structure: [What it is] [Key insight] [When to use]

title: Add 'You already use this' real-world mapping for each pattern
description: CONCEPT AUDIT: T9 (Real-World) scores 2/3 — examples exist but don't connect to what students ALREADY know. Add a 'youAlreadyUseThis' field mapping each pattern to frameworks/tools students use daily. EXAMPLE — Observer: 'React useEffect + setState = Observer. addEventListener in DOM = Observer. Redux store.subscribe() = Observer. If you've used ANY of these, you already know Observer.' Strategy: 'Express middleware choosing between different auth strategies = Strategy. Sorting with a custom comparator = Strategy.' This creates an instant 'oh I DO know this!' moment.
acceptanceCriteria: DesignPattern type has 'youAlreadyUseThis: string[]' field
All 20 patterns map to 2-4 frameworks/tools students already use
Maps cover React, Express, Python Django, Java Spring, and general (DOM, Array methods)
Displayed in Properties panel as 'You Already Use This' section with framework icons
Each mapping explains the connection in under 10 words

title: Add problem-first hooks to all 10 LLD problems
description: CONCEPT AUDIT: LLD problems score 1/3 on T1. Descriptions are concrete but don't motivate WHY this is interesting. Rewrite each to open with a scale/interview context. EXAMPLE — Parking Lot current: 'Design a parking lot system that supports multiple floors...' → Rewrite: 'Uber manages 50,000 parking spots across 200 cities. Every second, hundreds of cars enter and exit. How do you design a system that assigns spots in milliseconds, handles payment, and never double-books a space? This is the #1 most-asked LLD interview question at Google, Amazon, and Uber.'
acceptanceCriteria: All 10 LLD problem descriptions start with a real-world scale/company context
Each mentions interview relevance ('asked at Google/Amazon/Meta')
Current requirements list preserved after the hook
At least 5 problems mention specific companies that use similar systems

title: Add sequence diagram step-by-step 'why' narration for all 5 examples
description: CONCEPT AUDIT: Sequence diagrams score 0/3 on T4 (Why). Messages show labels ('GET /api/users', 'SELECT * FROM users') but never explain WHY each step happens in this order. Add 'stepNarration' field to each message. EXAMPLE — HTTP Request message 2 ('server → db: SELECT * FROM users'): narration = 'The server doesn't store user data itself — it delegates to the database. This separation of concerns means we can swap the database without changing the server.'
acceptanceCriteria: SequenceMessage type extended with optional 'narration: string' field
All 5 sequence examples have narration for every message
Narration explains WHY this step happens, not just WHAT
Narration displayed in Properties panel when a message is clicked
Each narration is 1-2 sentences, no jargon

title: Create content style guide enforcing hook-analogy-diagram-code-summary structure
description: SYSTEMIC FIX: The root cause of all F grades is that content was authored as REFERENCE (definition → UML → code) instead of TEACHING (hook → intuition → diagram → code → summary). Create a content style guide document that defines the required structure for every pattern/demo/problem. This prevents future content from falling into the same trap. The guide becomes the template for all new patterns added (LLD-091 to LLD-098).
acceptanceCriteria: Style guide document created at docs/content-style-guide.md
Required structure defined: Hook → Analogy → UML Diagram → Code → Tradeoffs → Summary
Each section has a template with placeholder text
Anti-patterns listed: 'Never start with a GoF definition', 'Never show UML before explaining why'
Guide referenced in CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md for future content PRs
Checklist for new pattern PRs: 'Does it have a hook? Analogy? 3-bullet summary?'

title: Add self-test prediction prompts to 5 key patterns
description: CONCEPT AUDIT: All concepts score 0/3 on T11 (Self-Test). No concept asks the student to PREDICT before seeing the answer. Add prediction prompts to the 5 most important patterns: Observer, Strategy, Factory Method, Decorator, Singleton. EXAMPLE — Observer: 'Before looking at the diagram: if you add a 6th observer to this system, how many classes need to change? (Answer: zero — that's the power of Observer)'. Strategy: 'If you need to add a new payment method (Bitcoin), how many existing classes need modification? Predict, then check the code.'
acceptanceCriteria: 5 key patterns have 'predictionPrompts: string[]' field with 1-2 prompts each
Prompts are displayed BEFORE the UML diagram with a 'Think first...' label
Each prompt has a hidden answer that can be revealed with a click
Prompts test the KEY INSIGHT of the pattern (decoupling, extensibility, etc.)
Prompts use 'How many classes change if you add X?' format for measurable prediction
